The integration of EDP-convergence concepts from the theory of gradient systems to control perturbations of attractors, rather than just energies, provides a new technical pathway.

proposals.title: Quasi-Potentials and Topological Descriptors for Non-Equilibrium Steady States in Non-Variational Pattern-Forming Systems
proposals.motivation: The rigorous comparison of solution structures and attractors in pattern-forming PDEs is well-established for gradient systems via energy landscapes but fails for non-variational systems like Kuramoto-Sivashinsky, which lack a direct Lyapunov function. This limits the understanding of pattern selection and stability under parameter changes. Methods from theoretical ecology, developed to analyze alternative stable states in complex, non-gradient systems, offer a promising but unexplored analogy for PDE analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: Computationally constructed quasi-potentials (via e.g., minimum action methods) and topological descriptors (like persistent homology of attractor basins) can provide a rigorous, quantitative framework for comparing non-equilibrium steady states and their resilience in non-variational pattern-forming PDEs, extending the attractor comparison paradigm.
proposals.method: We will adapt the 'Freidlin-Wentzell' quasi-potential construction, using geometric minimum action methods (gMAM) for stochastic PDEs in the zero-noise limit, to map effective landscapes for deterministic systems like FitzHugh-Nagumo. Concurrently, we will apply topological data analysis (TDA), specifically persistent homology, to computational approximations of attractor basins obtained from long-time simulations. The upper semicontinuity framework for gradient systems will guide the comparison of these landscape and topological metrics across parameter variations, establishing a convergence theory for the non-variational case.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new analytical and computational toolkit for characterizing and comparing complex solution structures in non-gradient PDEs, bridging ecological stability theory and applied analysis. It will extend Mielke's attractor comparison framework to a broader class of physically relevant pattern-forming equations, enabling a more systematic study of pattern selection and metastability.
proposals.novelty: The direct translation of ecological 'landscape' and topological methods to the rigorous analysis of PDE attractors is novel. Applying the minimum action method and persistent homology specifically for the purpose of establishing an attractor comparison theory in non-variational systems moves beyond existing numerical explorations.

proposals.title: Upper Semicontinuity of Pullback Attractors for Pattern-Forming Gradient Systems Under Non-Autonomous Perturbations
proposals.motivation: The stability of patterns in reaction-diffusion and convection systems under constant parameter changes is well-studied via attractor continuity for autonomous equations. However, realistic environments involve time-dependent forcing (e.g., periodic, quasi-periodic, or stochastic), which breaks autonomy and challenges classical theory. A rigorous framework for upper semicontinuity in such non-autonomous settings is crucial for understanding pattern resilience and transitions in geophysical, biological, and chemical systems subject to external drivers.
proposals.hypothesis: For a broad class of pattern-forming gradient or gradient-like systems, the pullback attractor of the time-perturbed equation converges upper semicontinuously to the global attractor of the limiting autonomous system as the time-dependent perturbation vanishes in a suitable topology, even when the limiting process is not simply an autonomous dynamical system.
proposals.method: The approach leverages the energetic structure of gradient systems, building on the EDP-convergence framework and the characterization of non-equilibrium steady states as saddle points. We will employ slow-fast decomposition techniques to analyze time-dependent perturbations as a slow modulation of parameters. Key steps involve establishing uniform dissipativity estimates for the non-autonomous semiflow, constructing a family of Lyapunov functions for the perturbed system, and applying the theory of pullback attractors. The convergence will be quantified using the Hausdorff semi-distance between the sections of the pullback attractor and the autonomous global attractor.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a general, rigorous criterion for pattern persistence under non-stationary environmental changes, directly applicable to models of physical pattern formation. It will extend the theory of attractor continuity beyond the standard autonomous perturbation paradigm, offering new tools for analyzing transitions in systems with time-dependent coefficients, such as climate-influenced convection or chemically reacting flows with oscillatory feed rates.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in systematically merging the theory of non-autonomous pullback attractors with the variational structure of gradient systems and EDP-convergence to handle perturbations that do not yield a simple autonomous limit, moving beyond the classical case of constant parameter shifts.

proposals.title: Rigorous Error Bounds for Attractor Approximation via EDP-Convergence and Relative Entropy
proposals.motivation: In the analysis of gradient and reaction-diffusion systems, attractors are often approximated via limiting procedures (e.g., slow-fast limits, singular perturbations). Pre-2026, while convergence results exist (e.g., Gamma-convergence of gradient structures), explicit, quantitative bounds on the approximation error in Hausdorff distance between attractors are largely missing. Such bounds are crucial for validating numerical and asymptotic model reduction schemes in non-equilibrium thermodynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: The distance between the global attractor of a full gradient or reaction-diffusion system and the attractor of its limiting system (e.g., a slow manifold or a coarse-grained model) can be explicitly bounded using tools from the Energy-Dissipation-Principle (EDP) convergence framework and statistical mechanics, specifically via the relative entropy (Kullback-Leibler divergence) between their invariant measures.
proposals.method: We will formulate the attractor approximation within the EDP-convergence framework for gradient systems, treating the limit attractor as an equilibrium state. The core method involves constructing a coarse-graining map between the full and reduced system states. We will then adapt techniques from statistical mechanics, using the relative entropy between the invariant (or Sinai-Ruelle-Bowen) measures supported on the respective attractors. By linking this entropy to the dissipation deficit in the EDP structure and exploiting spectral properties (like the system's spectral gap or Lyapunov exponents), we will derive explicit bounds on the Hausdorff distance as a function of the approximation parameter (e.g., the time-scale separation epsilon).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first general, quantitative error bounds for attractor approximation in slow-fast and singularly perturbed gradient systems. It will bridge the gap between the qualitative convergence theory of dynamical systems and the quantitative error analysis common in statistical mechanics and numerical analysis, offering a practical tool for certifying model reduction in dissipative PDEs.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious synthesis lies in applying the conceptual framework of coarse-graining error bounds from equilibrium statistical mechanics to the non-equilibrium, dynamical context of attractors for dissipative PDEs, leveraging Mielke's recent work on EDP-convergence and non-equilibrium steady states as saddle points.

proposals.title: Quantitative Semicontinuity of Attractors: Error Bounds as Signatures of Critical Transitions in Gradient Systems
proposals.motivation: The theory of attractor continuity under parameter perturbations is fundamental for understanding the robustness of models in reaction-diffusion systems and slow-fast gradient flows. While upper semicontinuity is well-understood, lower semicontinuity and quantitative error estimates are more delicate, often failing precisely at bifurcation points. A pre-2026 challenge is to move beyond qualitative statements to provide explicit, computable bounds that diagnose the dynamical mechanisms behind approximation failures.
proposals.hypothesis: The quantitative deviation from lower semicontinuity of attractors, under a parameter perturbation ε→0, can be bounded by a function that explicitly depends on the distance of the system to critical transitions, such as saddle-node bifurcations or collisions with unstable invariant sets. This bound serves as a diagnostic signature for the underlying dynamical obstruction.
proposals.method: We will leverage the energetic structure of gradient systems, building on the EDP-convergence framework and the characterization of non-equilibrium steady states as saddle points. The approach involves: 1) Using the energy-dissipation structure to derive Lyapunov-function-based distance estimates between attractors for perturbed and limiting systems. 2) Quantifying the gap in the energy landscape that signals a bifurcation (e.g., a vanishing spectral gap or collision of critical manifolds) and incorporating this into the error bound via a modulus of continuity. 3) Applying this to concrete slow-fast reaction-diffusion systems to obtain explicit rates conditional on the presence of a stable normally hyperbolic critical manifold.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, mechanism-aware theory for attractor approximation, yielding not just error estimates but also a practical diagnostic tool for detecting incipient bifurcations. It will significantly advance the understanding of attractor robustness in applied gradient systems, with direct implications for the analysis of numerical approximations and singular limits.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of quantitative error control with bifurcation diagnostics within the rigorous framework of EDP-convergence is novel. Existing work typically treats continuity and bifurcation separately, whereas this proposal intrinsically links the failure of lower semicontinuity to computable dynamical signatures.

proposals.title: Lower Semicontinuity of Attractors for Slow-Fast Reaction-Diffusion Systems via EDP-Convergence
proposals.motivation: In the analysis of dissipative gradient systems, the convergence of global attractors under parameter variation is crucial for understanding the robustness of long-term dynamics. While upper semicontinuity is well-studied, establishing lower semicontinuity—ensuring that all limiting dynamics are approximated by the perturbed system—is a more delicate problem, especially for systems with multiple time scales. This is central to validating reduced models in non-equilibrium thermodynamics and pattern formation, key themes in Mielke's recent work on slow-fast systems and similarity profiles.
proposals.hypothesis: For a family of gradient systems exhibiting a clear slow-fast structure, the convergence in the sense of the Energy-Dissipation Principle (EDP) provides sufficient structural control to prove the lower semicontinuity of their global attractors under the singular limit.
proposals.method: We will focus on a concrete class of coupled parabolic reaction-diffusion systems with a small parameter ε separating time scales. Building on Mielke's framework of EDP-convergence and the characterization of non-equilibrium steady states as saddle points, we will first establish the convergence of the associated energy and dissipation functionals in the sense of Γ-convergence. We will then employ the gradient structure to construct a parametrized family of slow manifolds and use shadowing techniques to track trajectories near the limiting attractor. The key technical step involves proving a stability property for the set of chain-recurrent points under the EDP-limit, leveraging the variational characterization of steady states.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, variational criterion for the lower semicontinuity of attractors, significantly strengthening the results of mere upper semicontinuity. It will yield a practical verification tool for complex systems, particularly in the context of pattern-forming systems where attractors are complex. The results will directly advance the theory of EDP-convergence by linking it to a stronger topological property of the dynamical system.
proposals.novelty: While EDP-convergence is known to imply convergence of equilibria and simple orbits, its sufficiency for the lower semicontinuity of the full attractor—a global object containing heteroclinic orbits and more complex invariant sets—is non-trivial and unexplored. The novel synthesis of variational slow-manifold techniques with the abstract EDP framework to address this problem is a distinct advance beyond existing perturbation theory.

proposals.title: Renormalization Group Flows for Gradient and Reaction-Diffusion Systems on Unbounded Domains: A Rigorous Approach via Weighted Sobolev Spaces
proposals.motivation: The analysis of long-time asymptotics and pattern formation in spatially extended systems on unbounded domains, such as reaction-diffusion equations, remains a fundamental challenge. Traditional renormalization group (RG) methods in statistical physics and dynamical systems often lack mathematical rigor, especially for infinite-dimensional phase spaces, relying on formal expansions or lattice approximations. A rigorous framework is needed to classify self-similar patterns and non-equilibrium steady states, which are central to Mielke's recent work on similarity profiles and slow-fast gradient systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A rigorous renormalization group transformation, defined via projections on carefully chosen weighted Sobolev spaces, will yield a well-defined flow on a space of effective dynamical systems. This RG flow will converge to fixed points that rigorously characterize the asymptotic attractors and critical behaviors of gradient and reaction-diffusion systems on R^d.
proposals.method: The core technical approach is to construct explicit renormalization maps as compositions of scaling and projection operators. We will define projections onto finite-dimensional subspaces of weighted Sobolev spaces W^{k,p}_μ(R^d), where the weight μ controls growth at infinity, ensuring the coarse-grained dynamics remain well-posed. The convergence of the RG iteration will be analyzed using the toolbox of evolutionary Gamma-convergence and the energy-dissipation-principle (EDP) framework, adapting Mielke's recent work on EDP-convergence for slow-fast systems. This will allow us to establish conditions under which the RG flow contracts to a fixed point describing a self-similar or steady-state profile.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first fully rigorous renormalization group framework for PDE dynamical systems on unbounded domains, free of artificial boundary conditions. It will yield new, provable criteria for the existence and stability of self-similar patterns and non-equilibrium steady states, directly applicable to gradient flows and reaction-diffusion systems. The results will bridge the gap between formal RG techniques in physics and the analytical methods of modern applied analysis.
proposals.novelty: The novel synthesis of rigorous renormalization with the analytical structure of weighted Sobolev spaces and the EDP-convergence framework is unprecedented. This moves beyond formal or perturbative RG schemes to provide a mathematically sound coarse-graining procedure that preserves the gradient or dissipative structure of the original infinite-dimensional system.

proposals.title: Gluing Far-Field Spatial Dynamics to Elliptic Cores: A Constructive Manifold Approach for Non-Monotone Patterns in Gradient Systems
proposals.motivation: Constructing localized patterns in reaction-diffusion systems on unbounded domains is a central challenge in non-equilibrium pattern formation. Standard spatial dynamics, which treats the unbounded spatial direction as a time-like variable, fails for non-monotone fronts or systems lacking a spectral gap, as the associated evolution is not well-posed. This causality flaw obstructs a rigorous dynamical systems framework for many physically relevant structures, such as multi-pulse solutions or patterns in systems with conservation laws.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid analytical framework, which decomposes the spatial domain into a far-field region governed by well-posed spatial dynamics and a core region treated with elliptic theory, can be rigorously glued via Lyapunov-Schmidt reduction to construct localized steady states without assuming global monotonicity or a spectral gap.
proposals.method: The domain is split at a large radius R. In the far-field (|x|>R), the system is formulated as a spatial dynamical system near a uniform state; a center-stable manifold is constructed, encoding radiation/decay conditions. In the core (|x|<R), the full elliptic system is solved. A gluing function smoothly transitions between regions. A Lyapunov-Schmidt reduction is applied to match the far-field manifold parameter (the 'scattering datum') with the core solution via continuity and derivative conditions at the interface, yielding a finite-dimensional equation for the existence of the global structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, well-posed, and constructive method for proving the existence of localized patterns in systems where classical spatial dynamics fails. It will extend the toolkit of geometric singular perturbation theory and EDP-convergence methods to a broader class of non-monotone spatial structures, with direct applications to slow-fast systems and non-equilibrium steady states identified as saddle points.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the explicit decoupling and rigorous matching of causal (far-field) and non-causal (core) spatial regions, bypassing the need for global evolutionary structure. This hybrid gluing approach is distinct from standard singular perturbation or center manifold techniques applied uniformly across the domain.

proposals.title: Spatial Dynamics and Center Manifolds for Non-Equilibrium Steady States in Coupled Reaction-Diffusion Systems
proposals.motivation: The analysis of self-similar patterns and non-equilibrium steady states (NESS) in parabolic systems on unbounded domains is a core challenge in non-equilibrium thermodynamics and pattern formation. Pre-2026 work by Mielke and others has established the existence of such states as saddle points in an energy-dissipation landscape, but a robust, dynamical-systems-based framework for their systematic construction and stability analysis in multi-component systems remains underdeveloped.
proposals.hypothesis: By reinterpreting the spatial coordinate in a similarity variable as a time-like variable, the search for self-similar profiles and NESS can be formulated as a heteroclinic or homoclinic connection problem in an infinite-dimensional spatial-dynamical system, where center manifold theory near degenerate spatial equilibria provides a rigorous reduction to finite dimensions.
proposals.method: The method applies spatial dynamics to the coupled parabolic systems studied in Mielke's recent papers. First, the system is rewritten in a traveling-wave or similarity coordinate, casting it as a first-order evolutionary system in the 'spatial time'. Linearizing around a spatially homogeneous or scale-invariant state identifies the essential spectrum. Near points where this spectrum has eigenvalues on the imaginary axis (spatial center directions), the theory of center manifolds for elliptic PDEs in infinite cylinders, as developed pre-2026, will be employed. This reduces the infinite-dimensional spatial flow to a finite-dimensional ordinary differential equation on the center manifold, where connecting orbits (profiles) can be analyzed using geometric singular perturbation theory, aligning with the slow-fast gradient system perspective.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, unified spatial-dynamical framework for proving the existence and multiplicity of self-similar profiles and NESS in gradient and non-gradient reaction-diffusion systems. It will directly extend the EDP-convergence and saddle-point theory for slow-fast systems to spatially extended patterns, offering a bridge between variational structures and dynamical systems techniques for pattern selection.
proposals.novelty: While spatial dynamics is classic for steady states, its rigorous application via center manifolds to the specific context of self-similar NESS in coupled parabolic systems, particularly those arising from gradient flows with dissipation, is novel. It moves beyond existence proofs via shooting methods or variational techniques to a dynamical classification of profile bifurcations.

proposals.title: A Dual Variational Principle for Non-Equilibrium Steady States in Gradient Systems via Legendre Transformation
proposals.motivation: In gradient systems, attractors like non-equilibrium steady states (NESS) are often characterized as minimizers of a Lyapunov functional, analogous to energy minimization in equilibrium thermodynamics. However, statistical mechanics shows that equilibrium states can also be characterized by dual principles, like minimizing free energy, which are more suitable under different constraints. Extending this duality to dynamical systems could yield new existence proofs and stability criteria for NESS in complex, slow-fast, or reaction-diffusion systems, offering a complementary perspective to direct minimization.
proposals.hypothesis: That a Legendre transformation applied to the Lyapunov functional of a gradient system, with respect to conjugate dynamical variables (e.g., parameters of invariant measures or slow manifold coordinates), yields a dual variational principle whose maximizers correspond to the system's attractors, particularly self-similar profiles and NESS.
proposals.method: We will first formalize conjugate variables for gradient flows with dissipation potentials, drawing on the Energy-Dissipation-Principle (EDP) framework. For a given Lyapunov functional, we will perform a partial Legendre transformation with respect to selected 'extensive' variables (e.g., related to the slow component in a slow-fast system). This constructs a dual functional. We will then prove, using convex analysis and Gamma-convergence techniques, that critical points (or maximizers) of this dual functional correspond to the original system's steady states. The approach will be tested on specific models from recent work, such as coupled parabolic systems admitting similarity profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous dual variational characterization of attractors in gradient systems, complementing the direct minimization approach. It will offer new tools for proving existence and stability of self-similar patterns and NESS, potentially simplifying analysis in systems with complex constraints. The principle may also lead to novel computational algorithms for locating attractors by maximizing a transformed quantity.
proposals.novelty: While Legendre transforms are standard in thermodynamics, their systematic application to construct dual variational principles for attractors in dissipative dynamical systems is novel. This moves beyond the established gradient-flow and EDP-convergence framework by introducing a conjugate, free-energy-like perspective for non-equilibrium states.

proposals.title: A Dual Variational Principle for Non-Equilibrium Steady States in Coupled Parabolic Systems
proposals.motivation: The existence of self-similar patterns and non-equilibrium steady states (NESS) in reaction-diffusion systems is a cornerstone of pattern formation theory, with applications from biology to materials science. While Mielke's recent work has established existence via a primal variational principle (saddle-point structure), a rigorous dual formulation is largely unexplored. Such a dual principle would provide a complementary characterization of NESS, potentially revealing new stability properties and enabling more efficient numerical computation of these complex states.
proposals.hypothesis: The primal variational characterization of NESS as saddle points of a specific energy-dissipation functional admits a mathematically rigorous and practically useful dual variational principle. This dual formulation will be convex and yield complementary bounds for the existence problem.
proposals.method: We will employ the framework of the Energy-Dissipation Principle (EDP) and convex analysis. Starting from the primal saddle-point structure for the slow-fast gradient system, we will apply Fenchel-Rockafellar duality to the integrated dissipation and energy functionals. The core technical step is to carefully identify the correct dual variables (e.g., generalized chemical potentials or fluxes) and the dual dissipation potential. The analysis will be applied to the specific parabolic systems studied in Mielke's recent papers on similarity profiles and coupled systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic dual variational principle for NESS in coupled parabolic systems, enriching the mathematical theory of gradient flows and dissipation. It will offer a new computational tool for verifying and locating self-similar patterns and may lead to sharper stability criteria based on dual gap functions.
proposals.novelty: While duality is classic in convex optimization, its application to the non-convex, saddle-point structure of NESS in slow-fast reaction-diffusion systems is novel. The approach moves beyond the primal EDP convergence framework to establish a complementary, potentially convex, variational description of these far-from-equilibrium states.

proposals.title: Upper Semicontinuity of Global Attractors for Generalized Gradient Systems: From Lattice Discretizations to Continuous Pattern-Forming PDEs
proposals.motivation: Numerical simulation of pattern-forming systems, such as reaction-diffusion equations and phase-field crystal models, is fundamentally performed on discrete spatial grids. A rigorous justification that the long-time dynamics (attractors) of these discrete approximations converge to those of the continuous PDE is largely missing for systems beyond simple scalar models. This gap limits the reliability of numerical predictions for pattern selection and stability in materials science and biology.
proposals.hypothesis: For a broad class of generalized gradient systems and reaction-diffusion equations on unbounded domains, the global attractors of their spatially discrete lattice approximations are upper semicontinuous with respect to the continuous attractor as the lattice spacing tends to zero.
proposals.method: The approach leverages the energetic structure of gradient flows and dissipation estimates, extending Mielke's work on EDP-convergence and slow-fast systems. We will first establish the existence of attractors for the infinite lattice systems using tail estimates and weighted spaces. The core technical step is to construct a suitable comparison metric between lattice and continuum functions, then prove a generalized Γ-convergence of the Lyapunov functionals combined with a suitable convergence of the dissipation potentials. This framework will be applied to specific systems like the phase-field crystal equation and coupled parabolic systems exhibiting self-similar patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous foundation for using discrete simulations to predict the asymptotic dynamics of continuous pattern-forming systems. It will extend the theory of attractor comparison to a new class of generalized gradient and reaction-diffusion systems, directly linking numerical analysis and infinite-dimensional dynamical systems.
proposals.novelty: The extension of attractor comparison to infinite lattices for systems beyond the Swift-Hohenberg equation, and the explicit fusion of EDP-convergence techniques with global attractor theory for pattern-forming PDEs, represent a significant and non-trivial advance.

proposals.title: Optimal Boundary Closures for Attractor Approximation: An Inverse Problem for Gradient and Reaction-Diffusion Systems on Finite Domains
proposals.motivation: Numerical analysis of pattern-forming systems on unbounded domains requires finite-domain truncations, which introduce boundary artifacts that can fundamentally alter the global attractor and steady states. Existing work on attractor approximation and EDP-convergence for gradient systems often treats boundaries as a necessary evil. This project addresses the inverse design problem: given a target attractor (e.g., a pattern from an unbounded system), what PDE and boundary conditions on a finite domain best preserve it?
proposals.hypothesis: For a broad class of gradient and parabolic reaction-diffusion systems, there exists a principled method to design boundary conditions and near-boundary modifications such that the finite-domain global attractor approximates a target attractor with higher fidelity than standard truncations, and these optimal 'closures' can be characterized via a variational principle related to the system's dissipation structure.
proposals.method: We will formulate the problem within the framework of gradient systems and the energy-dissipation principle (EDP), extending Mielke's work on EDP-convergence. The core is an inverse optimization: minimize a metric on the distance between attractors (e.g., using Hausdorff distance or a functional on steady states) over a parameterized family of boundary conditions and spatially-dependent mobility or potential modifications near the boundary. Analytical work will focus on model systems like the Allen-Cahn and Swift-Hohenberg equations, employing techniques from Gamma-convergence and singular perturbation theory to analyze the limit of large domain size. Numerical continuation methods will be used to trace branches of non-equilibrium steady states and compute attractors for candidate boundary designs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous methodology for designing computational domains that minimize boundary-induced dynamical distortion, directly applicable to the simulation of localized structures and self-similar patterns. It will establish new connections between the theory of attractors, inverse problems for PDEs, and the analysis of boundary effects, leading to concrete criteria for 'optimal' numerical boundary treatments in pattern-forming systems.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is treating the boundary not as a fixed constraint but as a design variable in an inverse problem for dynamical approximation, explicitly linking the analysis of boundary layers in extended systems to the fidelity of global attractor approximation.

proposals.title: Non-Convex Elastic Domains as Non-Equilibrium Steady States in a Generalized Gradient System Framework
proposals.motivation: Classical plasticity theory relies on convex elastic domains to ensure well-posedness and uniqueness, but this fails to capture complex yielding phenomena like shear banding, phase transformations, and jerky flow observed in amorphous solids and certain alloys. A rigorous mathematical framework for non-convex yield surfaces, grounded in variational structures, is lacking. This gap limits the predictive modeling of spatially heterogeneous, rate-dependent inelastic behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: Complex yielding patterns in materials can be modeled as stable, self-similar non-equilibrium steady states (NESS) of a generalized gradient system with a non-convex dissipation potential, where the non-convexity of the elastic domain emerges dynamically from the coupling of slow plastic flow and fast elastic relaxation.
proposals.method: We will formulate a coupled parabolic system for stress and internal variables as a generalized gradient flow (EDP structure) with a non-quadratic, non-convex dissipation potential. The method leverages Mielke's recent work on EDP-convergence for slow-fast systems and similarity profiles. We will analyze the existence of NESS via a time-scale separation, treating the slow plastic yielding as a driving force for the fast diffusion of stress. The central technique involves seeking self-similar solutions to the system and proving their stability as saddle points in an extended energy-dissipation landscape.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous variational justification for non-convex yield criteria within the GENERIC/EDP framework. It will deliver explicit conditions under which complex, localized yielding patterns emerge as mathematically stable structures, directly linking microscopic non-associativity to macroscopic instability patterns. This bridges the gap between phenomenological non-convex plasticity and the mathematics of dissipative systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in interpreting non-convex elastic domains not as a static modeling assumption but as the emergent macroscopic signature of a metastable NESS in a multi-scale gradient flow. This moves beyond ad-hoc non-convex potentials by deriving their structure from the interaction of time scales and reaction-diffusion coupling.

proposals.title: Non-Equilibrium Steady States and Metastability in Gradient Systems with Multi-Well Entropies
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work on gradient systems and the Energy-Dissipation-Principle (EDP) convergence for slow-fast systems largely relies on convex entropy functionals. Many physically relevant systems, such as phase-separating materials described by Cahn-Hilliard or Allen-Cahn dynamics, involve non-convex or multi-well entropy densities. A rigorous analysis of how such non-convexities affect the existence and stability of non-equilibrium steady states (NESS) and the structure of slow manifolds is a critical open problem.
proposals.hypothesis: For gradient systems with multi-well entropy functionals, the limiting slow dynamics on the non-equilibrium steady state manifold can be described by a generalized gradient flow, where the effective dissipation potential and driving force emerge from a homogenization of the fast phase-separation process.
proposals.method: We will employ the framework of EDP-convergence and evolutionary Gamma-convergence, extending it to functionals lacking global convexity. The core technical step involves a careful analysis of the saddle-point structure of the NESS in the fast subsystem, using tools from geometric singular perturbation theory for gradient flows. This will be applied to concrete reaction-diffusion systems with a non-convex logarithmic entropy, where we will rigorously derive an effective coarse-grained gradient flow on the space of averaged concentrations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous mathematical link between microscopic phase-separation dynamics and macroscopic effective gradient structures. It will extend the theory of slow-fast gradient systems to a fundamental class of non-convex problems, offering new insights into the metastable dynamics of pattern-forming systems far from equilibrium.
proposals.novelty: While EDP-convergence is established for convex settings, its application to multi-well entropies, where the fast dynamics induce a non-trivial effective metric on the slow manifold, is novel. The analysis of NESS as saddle points in a non-convex landscape moves beyond the standard convexity assumptions prevalent in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Gradient Structures with Spatially Dependent Mobility: EDP-Convergence and Pattern Formation in Reaction-Diffusion Systems
proposals.motivation: The theory of gradient flows in the Wasserstein metric has been pivotal for analyzing reaction-diffusion systems, but it typically assumes a constant mobility coefficient, simplifying the dissipation metric to the 2-Wasserstein distance. In many physical systems, such as those with porous media or non-ideal interactions, the mobility depends explicitly on spatial location. A rigorous generalization of the gradient structure to include spatially dependent mobilities is needed to model such systems accurately within the energetic framework of the Energy-Dissipation Principle (EDP).
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a generalized gradient structure with a spatially dependent mobility can be rigorously formulated, leading to a well-posed metric that supports EDP-convergence results. Furthermore, this structure will naturally capture spatially heterogeneous, self-similar patterns as non-equilibrium steady states in coupled parabolic systems.
proposals.method: We will define a Riemannian metric on the space of probability measures induced by the operator ρ ↦ −∇·(m(x)ρ∇(δE/δρ)), where m(x) > 0 is the mobility. The associated variational structure will be developed using the pre-2026 toolbox of EDP-convergence and Gamma-convergence for gradient systems. We will apply this framework to specific slow-fast reaction-diffusion systems, extending Mielke's recent work on saddle-point structures for NESS. Analytical techniques will focus on proving existence of solutions and similarity profiles for the resulting nonlinear diffusion equations.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend the geometric-variational analysis of dissipative systems to a broader class of physically relevant models. It will provide a new functional framework for studying pattern formation in heterogeneous media, directly linking spatially dependent transport coefficients to the existence and stability of self-similar non-equilibrium steady states.
proposals.novelty: While variable mobilities appear in PDE models, their consistent embedding into the variational structure of gradient flows and the subsequent analysis of EDP-convergence for coupled systems with such metrics is largely unexplored. The novelty lies in merging this geometric generalization with the analysis of slow-fast systems and pattern formation.

proposals.title: Differential Convexity and Gradient Structures for Complex Reaction-Diffusion Networks with Nonlinear Kinetics
proposals.motivation: The analysis of reaction-diffusion systems, particularly the existence and stability of non-equilibrium steady states (NESS) like self-similar patterns, is central to understanding pattern formation in biology and chemistry. While recent work by Mielke and others has successfully applied the Energy-Dissipation-Principle (EDP) and differential convexity methods to systems with simple, linear reaction terms, many physically relevant models involve complex, nonlinear multi-species kinetics (e.g., enzymatic or autocatalytic reactions). Extending these powerful geometric variational methods to such systems remains a significant open challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a broad class of reaction-diffusion systems with nonlinear, mass-action-type reaction kinetics can be rigorously formulated as generalized gradient systems, and that the framework of differential convexity (\textbackslash usepackage{amsmath}) can be adapted to prove the existence and saddle-point characterization of NESS for these more complex networks.
proposals.method: The project will systematically extend the differential convexity approach. First, we will formulate the reaction-diffusion system with nonlinear kinetics as a generalized gradient flow in the space of concentrations, using the Onsager operator and dissipation potential from chemical reaction network theory. The core technical work will involve analyzing the second variation of the corresponding entropy-entropy production structure to establish a suitable notion of uniform convexity or \emph{}\emph{}-convexity along trajectories. This will require careful decomposition of the reaction flux Jacobian and coupling estimates with the diffusion operator. The existence of NESS will then be studied via the saddle-point structure of the limiting energy-dissipation functional in the slow-fast scaling limit.
proposals.contribution: This work will significantly expand the applicability of the EDP-convergence framework to biologically and chemically realistic models. It will provide new analytical tools for proving the existence of patterned steady states in systems with complex nonlinear interactions, moving beyond the current theory which is largely restricted to linear or near-equilibrium reactions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of the abstract differential convexity methods from gradient systems theory with the specific structural properties of nonlinear chemical reaction networks, which possess their own inherent convexity (e.g., log-barrier terms) but present new challenges due to non-quadratic dissipation.

proposals.title: Explicit Exponential Convergence Rates for Reaction-Diffusion Systems via Geodesic λ-Convexity
proposals.motivation: In the analysis of gradient systems and non-equilibrium steady states (NESS), establishing convergence to equilibria or NESS is a central goal. While the existence of such convergence is often proven using entropy methods or gradient structures, quantitative rates, especially explicit ones, are less common. For slow-fast systems and coupled parabolic systems, as studied by Mielke and collaborators, deriving explicit decay rates would significantly strengthen the understanding of the approach to self-similar patterns and other NESS.
proposals.hypothesis: The proven geodesic λ-convexity of the driving entropy or free energy functional for certain reaction-diffusion systems and slow-fast gradient flows can be leveraged to derive explicit, computable exponential decay rates for solutions towards their steady states.
proposals.method: The project will focus on specific model systems from recent work, such as coupled parabolic systems admitting similarity profiles. The core technical approach is to rigorously connect the established lower bound on the geodesic convexity (λ) to the dissipation of the entropy functional via the Energy-Dissipation Principle (EDP). This involves a detailed analysis of the metric induced by the dissipation potential. The method will then apply the abstract theory of gradient flows in metric spaces, where λ-convexity implies exponential decay of the distance between the solution and the equilibrium at a rate directly given by λ.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first explicit formulas for convergence rates in several key models of reaction-diffusion and slow-fast gradient systems. It will bridge the gap between abstract convexity proofs and quantitative asymptotic analysis, offering a practical tool for comparing the relaxation timescales of different patterns and steady states.
proposals.novelty: While λ-convexity is a known sufficient condition for exponential decay, explicitly calculating the rate λ for the complex, nonlinear metrics arising in reaction-diffusion systems and translating it into a rate for the solution's profile is a non-trivial and largely open problem. This moves beyond existence proofs to deliver quantitative information.

proposals.title: Discrete Exterior Calculus for Structure-Preserving Discretization of Generalized Gradient Systems
proposals.motivation: The accurate numerical simulation of gradient flows and reaction-diffusion systems, central to Mielke's work on non-equilibrium steady states, requires schemes that preserve geometric structures like the underlying variational derivative and dissipation metric. Standard discretizations often violate these structures, leading to spurious long-time dynamics and inaccurate approximations of slow manifolds and saddle points. Discrete Exterior Calculus (DEC) provides a principled framework for preserving topological and geometric properties on discrete meshes, but its application to the generalized gradient systems of Mielke's 'metric' or 'EDP' framework remains unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a DEC-based spatial discretization, combined with a suitable time discretization, can yield numerical schemes for generalized gradient systems that exactly preserve the chain rule for the energy-dissipation balance at the discrete level. This will ensure robust convergence to correct non-equilibrium steady states and accurate approximation of slow-fast dynamics.
proposals.method: We will formulate the generalized gradient system ∂t u = -K(u) Dφ(u) within the DEC framework on simplicial complexes. The state variable u will be treated as a primal or dual discrete differential form. The Onsager operator K(u) will be discretized as a discrete Hodge star operator, possibly nonlinear and state-dependent, mapping co-differentials to vector fields. The driving functional φ(u) will be discretized using discrete exterior derivatives and Whitney forms to ensure a discrete variational derivative satisfies a discrete chain rule. The time discretization will employ an implicit, energy-stable scheme (e.g., minimizing movement) compatible with the DEC spatial structure. We will test the method on model problems from Mielke's recent papers, such as coupled parabolic systems exhibiting self-similar patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new class of structure-preserving numerical schemes for generalized gradient flows, ensuring discrete versions of the Energy-Dissipation Principle (EDP). It will extend the applicability of DEC beyond Hamiltonian and simple diffusion systems to the nonlinear, metric-driven systems central to modern non-equilibrium thermodynamics. The resulting numerical tools will enable more reliable studies of pattern formation, slow-fast dynamics, and convergence to non-equilibrium steady states.
proposals.novelty: While DEC has been used for Hamiltonian systems and simple gradient flows, its synthesis with the abstract 'metric' gradient system framework of Mielke et al. is novel. The key innovation is the discrete representation of the Onsager operator via DEC's Hodge theory to preserve the metric structure of the dissipation, not just the topological structure of the energy.

proposals.title: Inverse Reconstruction of Entropy-Driven Dynamics: Identifying Entropy Functionals from Reaction-Diffusion Patterns
proposals.motivation: In gradient systems and reaction-diffusion equations, the entropy functional governs the long-time dynamics, pattern formation, and selection of non-equilibrium steady states. However, for complex systems, the correct entropy is often unknown or postulated phenomenologically. An inverse methodology to deduce the entropy from observed spatiotemporal evolution would provide a fundamental data-driven link between macroscopic patterns and their underlying thermodynamic structure, crucial for validating models in biology and materials science.
proposals.hypothesis: Given sufficiently rich observational data of a system's evolution towards a non-equilibrium steady state, the driving entropy functional and its dissipation potential can be uniquely reconstructed by formulating and solving a variational inverse problem within the framework of the Energy-Dissipation Principle (EDP).
proposals.method: We will formulate the inverse problem as a PDE-constrained optimization. For a candidate entropy functional E(u) and dissipation potential Ψ(u,u̇), we minimize the discrepancy between simulated dynamics (solving the gradient flow ∂t u = -DΨ* (DE(u))) and observed data. The core algorithm involves an adjoint-based gradient descent on the space of functionals, parameterizing E(u) via neural networks or spline bases to ensure convexity. The approach will be tested on known systems (e.g., the Allen-Cahn or porous medium equations) before application to coupled parabolic systems exhibiting self-similar patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a rigorous computational framework for the inverse identification of thermodynamic driving functionals. It will extend the theory of gradient systems by providing a method to empirically validate or discover entropy structures, directly linking the abstract EDP-convergence theory to observable pattern dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While the forward theory of gradient flows is mature, the systematic inverse problem of reconstructing the entropy from dynamics is largely unexplored. The novelty lies in merging Mielke's EDP formalism with modern PDE-constrained optimization to solve this identification problem, moving beyond model fitting to functional reconstruction.

ideas.title: Dual Variational Principle for Attractors via Thermodynamic Free Energy Analogy
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution strategy from statistical mechanics, where equilibrium states are characterized by dual variational principles (e.g., minimizing free energy instead of energy), to the context of attractor existence in dynamical systems. The analogous thermodynamic solution involves performing a Legendre transformation on the fundamental equation to switch between extensive and intensive variables, yielding a dual variational principle valid under different constraints. The adapted idea is to formulate a dual variational principle for the attractor existence theorem by identifying conjugate dynamical variables (such as those related to invariant measures, Lyapunov exponents, or topological entropy) and constructing a Legendre-transformed functional. This would provide a complementary characterization of attractors, potentially offering new existence proofs, stability criteria, or computational methods based on maximizing a transformed quantity rather than minimizing the original Lyapunov functional.

ideas.title: Adapting Astrophysical Stability Analysis to Weakly Dissipative PDEs
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution strategy from astrophysics to challenge the strong dissipativity assumption in attractor theory for PDEs on unbounded domains. In astrophysics, systems like star clusters or dark matter halos exist on unbounded domains without conventional dissipation; their long-term boundedness is often proven using virial theorems that leverage conserved quantities and system constraints. The adapted idea is to develop an analogous 'functional virial theorem' for partial differential equations. This involves identifying suitable energy-like functionals whose time derivatives, when combined via a virial-type identity, can demonstrate uniform ultimate boundedness of solutions. The goal is to establish sufficient conditions for attractor existence that replace strict dissipativity with a balance condition derived from the system's intrinsic structure, potentially expanding the theory to non-gradient, weakly dissipative, or certain conservative-perturbed systems.

ideas.title: A Meta-Framework for Attractor Comparison: Classifying Systems for Valid Semicontinuity Methods
ideas.description: This research develops a classification scheme for pattern-forming PDEs based on the structural properties of their global attractors, such as finite fractal dimension, gradient structure, and symmetry. It rigorously derives the necessary and sufficient conditions—concerning phase space embeddings, perturbation types, and continuity of solution maps—for the valid application of upper semicontinuity comparison techniques. The method is then demonstrably applied to a curated hierarchy of systems, from conserved phase-field models to reaction-diffusion systems, proving the robustness of the comparisons and preventing the method's misapplication.

ideas.title: Dual Variational Principles for Strongly Unstable Systems: A Generalized Ginzburg-Landau Approach
ideas.description: This research direction synthesizes the extension of Ginzburg-Landau-type formalisms to strongly unstable or non-parabolic systems with the formulation of a dual variational principle for attractor existence. The core synergy is to develop a dual variational principle specifically tailored for systems far from equilibrium, where the standard Lyapunov functional is ill-behaved or non-existent. This dual formulation would provide a complementary minimization problem whose solution corresponds to a generalized 'attractor state', potentially enabling the analysis of pattern selection, defect dynamics, and statistical descriptors in turbulent or chaotic regimes. The result is a novel analytical and computational tool to probe the structure of complex attractors in fluid dynamics, nonlinear optics, and active matter, without relying on weak instability assumptions.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Stability Landscape Methods to Pattern-Forming PDEs
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from theoretical ecology, where researchers analyze alternative stable states in complex ecosystems using potential-like landscape methods and topological data analysis when direct Lyapunov functions are unavailable. The analogous ecological solution involves constructing 'quasi-potentials' or using persistent homology to compare attractor basins and resilience across diverse ecosystem models. Adapted to pattern-forming PDEs, this approach involves applying the upper semicontinuity comparison method to non-variational systems like the Kuramoto-Sivashinsky equation or reaction-diffusion models (e.g., FitzHugh-Nagumo) by computationally constructing quasi-potentials or using topological descriptors (like persistent homology) to characterize attractors. This would extend the attractor comparison framework beyond gradient systems like Swift-Hohenberg and Ginzburg-Landau to a broader class of pattern-forming equations, enabling rigorous comparison of their solution structures under parameter variation.

ideas.title: Quantifying attractor approximation error via statistical mechanics coarse-graining bounds
ideas.description: This research adapts solution strategies from statistical mechanics, where rigorous error bounds are derived for coarse-grained approximations of equilibrium states, to the problem of quantifying attractor approximation error in dynamical systems. The analogous problem involves bounding the distance between a true microscopic equilibrium and its macroscopic approximation using tools like relative entropy or Wasserstein metrics. The adapted idea treats the limit attractor in a semicontinuity context as the 'true equilibrium' and the approximating attractors as 'coarse-grained states'. The goal is to develop explicit, computable bounds—potentially using Lyapunov exponents, spectral gaps, or discrepancies between invariant measures—that quantify the Hausdorff distance between attractors as a function of the approximation parameter, thereby providing a rigorous error analysis for attractor approximation schemes.

ideas.title: Lower Semicontinuity of Quantum Attractors via Coarse-Grained Phase-Space Distributions
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the conditions for lower semicontinuity in the convergence of quantum attractors, such as invariant measures or asymptotic states in open quantum systems, as system parameters are varied. The key innovation is to characterize these attractors and their stability using Husimi (or other smoothed) phase-space distributions instead of the Wigner function, as their non-negativity and regularity provide a robust, coarse-grained geometric description of the asymptotic state. This framework allows one to prove that if a phase-space region is populated in the limit of some parameter sequence, it must also be populated in the limit attractor, by leveraging the continuous dependence of the Husimi distribution on both the quantum state and the parameter. The synergy creates a powerful analytical tool for semiclassical analysis where the continuity properties of attractors are tied directly to observable, smooth phase-space densities.

ideas.title: Hybrid Far-Field/Core Manifold Gluing for Non-Monotonic Spatial Dynamics
ideas.description: This approach decomposes the spatial domain into distinct regions to overcome the causality flaw in standard spatial dynamics. In the far-field, where the system approaches a uniform state, a spatial dynamics formulation is valid and a center-stable manifold can be constructed, treating the direction as time-like with imposed radiation/decay conditions. This far-field manifold is then rigorously matched, via a gluing function and Lyapunov-Schmidt reduction, to a solution in the non-causal core region where full elliptic theory is applied. The result is a well-posed, constructive method for finding localized structures that does not assume global monotonicity in the unbounded direction.

ideas.title: Spatial Dynamics on Non-Cylindrical Unbounded Domains for Attractor Theory
ideas.description: This research direction synthesizes spatial dynamics with attractor theory on general unbounded domains. By parameterizing the domain's non-cylindrical structure (e.g., cones, paraboloids, or diverging channels) and treating the primary unbounded direction as a time-like variable, one can reformulate a parabolic PDE as an evolutionary system in an abstract space of cross-sectional functions. The core synergy is applying center manifold theory to this spatial dynamical system to reduce the infinite-dimensional asymptotic behavior near a critical point to a finite-dimensional ordinary differential equation on the manifold, even when the domain's cross-section varies. This provides a powerful framework to classify and construct patterns, fronts, and attractors tailored to the domain's geometry, moving far beyond the classical cylindrical case.

ideas.title: Rigorous Renormalization Group Framework for Dynamical Systems on Unbounded Domains via Weighted Function Spaces
ideas.description: This research develops a mathematically rigorous renormalization group methodology for analyzing attractors in dynamical systems defined on unbounded domains. The approach constructs explicit renormalization transformations using projection operators on weighted Sobolev spaces, ensuring well-defined coarse-graining procedures that maintain key dynamical properties across scales. The framework establishes convergence criteria for RG flows in infinite-dimensional phase spaces and provides systematic classification of asymptotic behaviors near attractors. This enables quantitative analysis of pattern formation, stability, and critical phenomena in spatially extended systems without artificial boundary conditions.

ideas.title: Inverse Attractor Approximation on Finite Domains: PDE Design for Controlled Boundary Effects
ideas.description: This research program studies the inverse problem of finding a family of PDEs on a large but finite domain whose global attractors approximate a given target attractor (possibly defined on an unbounded domain). The core novelty is to treat the domain boundary not just as a truncation artifact but as a design parameter in the inverse problem, investigating how different boundary conditions and near-boundary modifications of the PDE influence the fidelity and structure of the finite-domain attractor approximation. This leads to questions about the optimal 'boundary closure' for an approximate PDE to preserve the essential dynamics, and how the required PDE family changes as the domain size grows, bridging approximation theory for attractors with the analysis of boundary effects in extended systems.

ideas.title: Quantitative Characterization of Attractor Semicontinuity: Bounds and Bifurcation Signatures
ideas.description: This research program aims to develop a comprehensive theory that quantifies the distance between attractors in the limit of parameter perturbations while simultaneously characterizing the conditions for lower semicontinuity. The core synergy lies in deriving error bounds that are explicitly conditional on the presence or absence of specific dynamical obstructions (like bifurcations or collisions with unstable invariant sets) that destroy lower semicontinuity. This approach will yield not just an error estimate, but a diagnostic tool: the quantitative deviation from lower semicontinuity serves as a signature for underlying critical transitions in the system. Consequently, the theory will provide a sharper, mechanism-aware understanding of attractor robustness and approximation limits in applied dynamical systems.

ideas.title: Upper Semicontinuity of Global Attractors Under Time-Dependent Perturbations in Pattern-Forming Systems
ideas.description: This research direction develops a theory of upper semicontinuity for global attractors applicable to a broad class of pattern-forming equations (e.g., reaction-diffusion, Boussinesq convection) subjected to time-dependent perturbations. It challenges the standard paradigm of time-independent parameter shifts by considering perturbations that may be periodic, quasi-periodic, or stochastic in time, which do not necessarily yield a simple autonomous limiting equation. The core synergy is establishing continuity criteria that connect the perturbed non-autonomous attractors or pullback attractors to the autonomous attractor of the limiting system, providing a rigorous tool to analyze pattern resilience and transitions in realistic, non-stationary environments.

ideas.title: Discrete Lattice Attractor Comparison for Generalized Pattern-Forming Systems
ideas.description: This research program develops a unified attractor comparison theory for pattern-forming partial differential equations (PDEs) and their discrete analogues on infinite lattices. It extends the upper semicontinuity comparison method beyond classical models like Swift-Hohenberg to systems such as reaction-diffusion equations, phase-field crystal models, and generalized gradient flows. By establishing precise convergence of global attractors as the lattice spacing vanishes, the work provides a rigorous foundation for using discrete simulations to predict the pattern selection and dynamical behavior of continuous systems. This synergy bridges numerical analysis and dynamical systems theory for a wide array of spatially extended systems.

ideas.title: Finite-Size Scaling Analysis for Dynamical Attractors Using Statistical Mechanics Methods
ideas.description: This research adapts the finite-size scaling methodology from statistical mechanics to the study of attractors in dynamical systems. The core problem is analogous: understanding how a property of interest (an attractor) on an infinite domain is approximated by studying it on successively larger finite domains. The analogous solution from critical phenomena involves running simulations or analyses on systems of size L, measuring key observables, and using scaling theory to extrapolate to L→∞. Adapted here, one would numerically compute invariant measures, fractal dimensions, or other attractor properties on bounded domains of increasing size. By analyzing how these quantities scale with domain size, one can construct scaling functions to predict the properties of the attractor on the unbounded domain, rigorously quantify the convergence rate, and identify potential finite-size artifacts in practical simulations of unbounded systems.

ideas.title: Adapting Geophysical Inverse Problem Regularization to Weighted Sobolev Localization
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution techniques from geophysical inverse problems, where weighted regularization norms are used to stabilize reconstructions in regions of poor data sensitivity, to the context of PDE analysis using weighted Sobolev spaces. In geophysics, a spatially varying weight within a Sobolev-type regularization term is constructed based on prior knowledge or sensitivity kernels to produce stable, physically plausible models. This approach is adapted by employing advanced weighted Sobolev space theory to refine the localizing norm approach for PDEs. Specifically, we propose to design the weight function in the Sobolev norm based on spectral or energetic properties of the differential operator (e.g., derived from its principal symbol or a related Lyapunov function). This will create a localizing norm that is inherently tuned to the operator's characteristics, amplifying sensitivity in critical regions (like near singularities or boundaries) and providing a more precise and stable tool for proving localized estimates, analogous to how geophysicists stabilize inversions in poorly constrained subsurface regions.

ideas.title: A Multi-Scale Framework for the Existence and Stability of Lyapunov Exponents Under General Time-Dependent Perturbations
ideas.description: This research develops a principled framework to classify time-dependent perturbations (stochastic, quasi-periodic, decaying) and analytically derive the conditions required for a well-defined asymptotic Lyapunov exponent to exist. It employs a combination of non-autonomous stability theory, random dynamical systems, and high-precision numerical continuation to map the boundaries of the 'Lyapunov regime.' The outcome will be a predictive taxonomy linking perturbation spectra and system nonlinearity to the validity of LE, directly challenging the default assumption of time-independent analysis in stability assessments.

ideas.title: Characterizing Function Space Geometry When Weighted Integrability Controls Uniform Boundedness
ideas.description: This research formalizes the inversion of uniform-local norm interactions by fixing the domain as a non-compact metric measure space (e.g., Euclidean space) and the weighted norm as a weighted L^p norm with a radial, polynomially decaying weight. The core problem is to determine necessary and sufficient conditions on the weight's decay rate and the exponent p such that the weighted norm bound implies a uniform (sup-norm) bound, effectively forcing the function to vanish at infinity. The approach involves analyzing the corresponding embedding operators, studying the compactness of such embeddings, and deriving precise regularity or decay profiles for functions in the intersection of these norms, thereby revealing the geometric constraints imposed by the dominance of the weighted integral.

ideas.title: Weighted Sobolev Spaces for Random Matrix Modeling of Localized Lyapunov Exponents
ideas.description: This research direction synthesizes random matrix theory (RMT) models for Lyapunov exponent (LE) statistics with the analytical control offered by weighted Sobolev spaces. The core idea is to define a weighted Sobolev norm tailored to the eigenvector statistics of the underlying random matrix ensemble, which governs the LE decay. This norm then provides a rigorous, quantitative measure for the localization of chaotic wavefunctions, allowing one to derive precise bounds on the tails of the LE distribution. Consequently, the approach moves beyond typical RMT averages to mathematically characterize the stability and transport properties associated with anomalous, non-ergodic trajectories in semiclassical limits.

ideas.title: Quantifying Finite-Size Dynamical Phases and Metastable Barriers in Spatially Extended Systems
ideas.description: This research systematically investigates how attractors on finite domains approximate their infinite-domain counterparts, with a focus on non-monotonic and pathologically slow convergence. It moves beyond simple size scaling to classify distinct finite-size dynamical phases—such as boundary-dominated, metastable, and bulk-like regimes—based on the interplay between intrinsic dynamics and boundary conditions. The approach combines mathematical analysis of edge modes and invariant manifolds with high-performance numerical bifurcation tracking to map phase boundaries in parameter-size space. The outcome is a predictive framework for estimating the domain sizes required to achieve a desired approximation fidelity and for identifying systems where the infinite-domain limit is effectively inaccessible due to persistent finite-size effects.

ideas.title: Adapting Renormalization Group Techniques from Quantum Field Theory to Strongly Unstable Ginzburg-Landau Systems
ideas.description: This idea challenges the weak instability assumption in the standard Ginzburg-Landau formalism by drawing an analogy from Quantum Field Theory. In QFT, the Renormalization Group (RG) is used to handle strongly interacting systems where perturbation theory fails, by deriving effective theories at different scales. The analogous solution is adapted by proposing an RG-inspired approach for strongly unstable or non-parabolic systems. The adapted research idea involves developing a renormalization procedure for the Ginzburg-Landau free energy, where strongly unstable modes are systematically integrated out to flow the system's parameters towards a fixed point that describes the effective, coarse-grained dynamics. This could reveal new universal classes for systems far from equilibrium or with strong nonlinear instabilities, moving beyond the traditional parabolic expansion.

ideas.title: Inverse Identification of Dynamical Generators from Attractor Data within Constrained PDE Classes
ideas.description: This research program addresses the inverse approximation problem by grounding it in a constrained optimization framework. Given a target attractor (typically from empirical data) and a predefined, physically-motivated class of PDEs (e.g., a parameterized family of Swift-Hohenberg or Keller-Segel type models), the goal is to identify the specific parameters and functional forms within that class whose global attractor best matches the target. The approach integrates statistical learning for parameter inference with dynamical systems theory to ensure the identified PDE not only replicates the attractor but also captures correct stability and bifurcation properties. The output is a validated, interpretable PDE model that explains the observed pattern formation or coherent structure with known physical mechanisms.

ideas.title: Attractor Reconstruction via Sparse Symbolic Regression
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to the network inference problem from systems biology to the inverse attractor approximation problem. In biology, one often has time-series data (an attractor) and must find the governing ODEs (the regulatory network) by searching a space of possible interaction terms using sparse regression. Here, we apply this to PDEs. Given a target attractor, we construct an extensive library of potential PDE terms—including various derivatives, polynomial nonlinearities, and non-local operators. Using a sparsity-promoting optimization technique (e.g., LASSO or sequential thresholding), we solve for the minimal-weight combination of these terms that yields a PDE whose own attractor approximates the target. This generates a scalable family of PDE models, where the sparsity constraint (lambda) controls the trade-off between approximation accuracy and model simplicity, directly addressing the scaling dual dimension of the original idea.

ideas.title: Proving Lower Semicontinuity of Attractors via Topological Persistence Methods
ideas.description: This research idea investigates conditions for the lower semicontinuity of attractors in dynamical systems by drawing an analogy from computational topology. The analogous problem is proving the stability of persistence diagrams in Topological Data Analysis (TDA), which is solved using interleaving distances and algebraic stability theorems for persistence modules. The adapted solution proposes to reframe a parametrized family of dynamical systems as generating a structure analogous to a filtration. By defining an appropriate interleaving distance between attractors—potentially based on a scale parameter measuring their containment within neighborhoods of one another—one can apply a similar algebraic topological framework. The core research aim is to establish that convergence in system parameters implies an interleaving relationship, which in turn guarantees lower semicontinuity of the attractor mapping, providing new, more algebraic and topological sufficient conditions for this classical dynamical systems property.

ideas.title: Adapting Adiabatic Invariants from Celestial Mechanics to Time-Dependent Lyapunov Analysis
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption of time-independent perturbations in Lyapunov exponent (LE) analysis by drawing an analogy from celestial mechanics. In orbital dynamics, the solution to a slowly time-dependent gravitational perturbation involves identifying adiabatic invariants—quantities that remain nearly constant, allowing for tractable long-term predictions. Adapting this solution, the research will investigate LE for time-dependent perturbations by seeking analogous slowly-varying stability quantities. It will establish conditions under which a perturbation is adiabatic relative to the system's divergence timescales and derive a framework for a 'Lyapunov regime' that is not a simple static snapshot but an evolving description of stability, moving beyond the limitations of time-independent theory.

ideas.title: Proving lower semicontinuity of quantum many-body attractors via tensor network methods
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the conditions for lower semicontinuity of attractors in spatially extended, chaotic quantum many-body systems. It employs tensor network simulations—specifically Matrix Product States (MPS) and Time-Dependent Variational Principle (TDVP) methods—to numerically construct and characterize non-equilibrium steady states and other dynamical attractors. The core synergy is using the efficient, structure-preserving representations of tensor networks not just for simulation, but to mathematically analyze how these attractor sets change under perturbations of system parameters, aiming to prove rigorous continuity theorems for quantum systems where traditional analytical approaches fail.

ideas.title: Structure-Informed Dynamic Sobolev Framework for Norm Localization
ideas.description: This approach constructs a localizing norm not from generic weighted Sobolev spaces, but from a data-guided, adaptive framework rooted in the spectral and pseudospectral properties of the linearized evolution operator. The method iteratively refines an initial energy-based norm using numerical approximations of the most unstable modes and pseudospectral contours, building a structure-informed Sobolev-type norm that directly targets the instability. This creates a mathematically rigorous, dynamically relevant norm whose geometric interpretation is clear and whose efficacy is validated through a feedback loop with nonlinear simulations.

ideas.title: Apply renormalization group methods to analyze attractors on unbounded domains using quantum field theory regularization techniques
ideas.description: This research adapts regularization techniques from quantum field theory to analyze dynamical attractors on unbounded domains using renormalization group methods. In quantum field theory, ultraviolet divergences in calculations over unbounded momentum spaces are systematically handled through regularization (e.g., momentum cutoffs, dimensional regularization) followed by renormalization group flow. The analogous problem in dynamical systems involves divergences or ill-defined properties when analyzing attractors on unbounded phase spaces. The adapted solution introduces a regularization parameter to make intermediate calculations finite, then applies renormalization group techniques to study how attractor characteristics (such as basin boundaries, stability properties, or invariant measures) scale as the regularization is systematically removed. This approach provides a rigorous framework to extract meaningful physical predictions from dynamical systems with unbounded domains, potentially revealing universal scaling behaviors near infinity that characterize the system's asymptotic dynamics.

ideas.title: Topological Embedding and Projection for Generalized Instability Analysis
ideas.description: This work addresses the fundamental incompatibility of classical amplitude equations with strong instabilities by introducing a two-stage topological methodology. First, the original system is embedded into a higher-parameter family where a well-defined, weakly unstable manifold exists, enabling a rigorous Ginzburg-Landau reduction. Second, a controlled singular projection maps the derived modulation dynamics back to the original parameter regime, preserving essential topological features of the solution set like defect classes and pattern selection. This provides a formally justified extension of envelope equation techniques to regimes where direct asymptotic expansion fails, offering new analytical tools for explosive pattern formation and transient chaotic structures.

ideas.title: Variational MPO Approximation of the Loschmidt Echo Operator for Extended Chaotic Systems
ideas.description: This project develops a tensor network method to compute the Loschmidt echo (LE) in many-body chaotic systems by directly approximating the echo operator as a matrix product operator (MPO). The core innovation is a variational algorithm that finds the best low-bond-dimension MPO representation of U†(t)U'(t), where U and U' are time-evolution operators under slightly different Hamiltonians. This approach sidesteps the entanglement growth limitation inherent in simulating the two time-evolved states separately. The method enables the calculation of the LE and its decay rate (related to the quantum Lyapunov exponent) for significantly longer times and larger systems than previously possible, providing a direct numerical probe of scrambling and chaos.

ideas.title: Spatial Dynamics of Localization Entropy Decay in Non-Hamiltonian Open Quantum Systems
ideas.description: This research direction treats the spatial progression of a quantum many-body system's correlations as a dynamical system in an abstract 'spatial' dimension, using center manifold theory near a critical point representing perfect localization. By introducing non-Hamiltonian perturbations—such as local dephasing, particle loss, or continuous measurement—as terms in this spatial dynamical system, we can study how they modify the stability of the localized manifold. This approach transforms the problem of calculating LE decay rates into analyzing the spectrum of a spatial evolution operator, where dissipative terms can induce novel transitions between algebraic, exponential, or even arrested decay. Consequently, the framework predicts how engineered non-unitary dynamics could be used to control information scrambling and protect localized phases in open systems.
